quiz_questions.question_text: Duchenne muscular dystrophy is caused by:

options.text: Muscle overuse from excessive exercise
A bacterial infection
A vitamin deficiency
A genetic mutation in the dystrophin gene (X-linked recessive)

quiz_questions.explanation: Duchenne muscular dystrophy is caused by mutations in the DMD gene on the X chromosome that lead to the absence of dystrophin, a critical protein that anchors the muscle fiber's cytoskeleton to the extracellular matrix, resulting in progressive muscle degeneration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Myasthenia gravis primarily affects the:

options.text: The intestinal smooth muscle
Bones and joints
The brain's motor cortex
Neuromuscular junction, where autoantibodies destroy ACh receptors

quiz_questions.explanation: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder in which the immune system produces antibodies that attack and destroy nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction, reducing the number of available receptors and causing progressive muscle weakness.

quiz_questions.question_text: Fibromyalgia is characterized by:

options.text: Bone fractures throughout the body
Visible muscle wasting and tissue destruction
Chronic widespread musculoskeletal pain and fatigue without identifiable tissue damage
Sudden acute muscle rupture

quiz_questions.explanation: Fibromyalgia is a chronic condition characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, sleep disturbances, and cognitive difficulties, with no identifiable structural tissue damage on examination or imaging, thought to involve abnormal central pain processing.

quiz_questions.question_text: A muscle strain involves damage to:

options.text: Overstretched or torn muscle fibers and/or tendons
A bone fracture
A ligament tear
A joint dislocation

quiz_questions.explanation: A muscle strain is an injury to muscle fibers or their associated tendons caused by overstretching or excessive force, ranging from mild microscopic fiber damage (Grade I) to complete rupture (Grade III) of the muscle-tendon unit.

quiz_questions.question_text: Duchenne muscular dystrophy primarily affects:

options.text: Adult men and women equally
Only newborn females
Boys, because it is X-linked recessive
Elderly women

quiz_questions.explanation: Because Duchenne MD is X-linked recessive, males (who have only one X chromosome) are predominantly affected, while females typically serve as carriers with two X chromosomes providing a backup copy of the dystrophin gene.

quiz_questions.question_text: Rhabdomyolysis can lead to which serious complication?

options.text: Stronger bones
Improved athletic performance
No significant complications
Acute kidney failure due to myoglobin release into the bloodstream

quiz_questions.explanation: When muscle fibers are severely damaged and break down (rhabdomyolysis), large quantities of myoglobin are released into the bloodstream; this protein can precipitate in and clog the renal tubules, leading to acute kidney injury or failure.

quiz_questions.question_text: Tetanus (the disease) is caused by a toxin from which organism?

options.text: Streptococcus pyogenes
Escherichia coli
Clostridium tetani
Staphylococcus aureus

quiz_questions.explanation: Tetanus is caused by the neurotoxin tetanospasmin produced by the anaerobic bacterium Clostridium tetani, which blocks inhibitory neurotransmitter release in the spinal cord, resulting in uncontrolled muscle contraction and spasms (lockjaw).

quiz_questions.question_text: DOMS (Delayed Onset Muscle Soreness) typically occurs:

options.text: 24-72 hours after unaccustomed or intense exercise
During exercise
Immediately and resolves within minutes
Only in professional athletes

quiz_questions.explanation: DOMS is delayed-onset muscle soreness that typically peaks 24-72 hours after exercise, especially eccentric exercise, and is caused by microscopic damage to muscle fibers that triggers an inflammatory repair response.

quiz_questions.question_text: Botulinum toxin (Botox) works by:

options.text: Stimulating acetylcholine release for stronger contractions
Blocking ACh release at the neuromuscular junction, causing localized muscle paralysis
Increasing calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum
Strengthening muscle fibers through protein synthesis

quiz_questions.explanation: Botulinum toxin blocks the release of acetylcholine from the presynaptic terminal at the NMJ by cleaving SNARE proteins required for vesicle fusion, causing temporary, localized muscle paralysis used therapeutically for spasticity, dystonia, and cosmetically for wrinkle reduction.

quiz_questions.question_text: The RICE protocol for acute muscle injuries stands for:

options.text: Resist, Inflate, Compress, Extend
Run, Ice, Contract, Elevate
Rehab, Inject, Cool, Exercise
Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation

quiz_questions.explanation: RICE (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation) is the standard first-aid protocol for acute musculoskeletal injuries that helps reduce swelling, inflammation, and pain in the initial 48-72 hours after injury.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is muscular dystrophy?

options.text: A condition where muscles grow too large from overuse
A temporary muscle cramp caused by dehydration
An infection of the muscle tissue by bacteria
A group of inherited genetic diseases characterized by progressive muscle weakness and degeneration due to defective structural proteins

quiz_questions.explanation: Muscular dystrophy encompasses a group of more than 30 inherited genetic disorders in which mutations in genes encoding structural muscle proteins (like dystrophin) lead to progressive skeletal muscle weakness, degeneration, and replacement with fibrotic and fatty tissue.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is atrophy in the context of muscular disorders?

options.text: A decrease in muscle size and strength resulting from disuse, denervation, aging, or disease
An increase in muscle size from exercise
The normal resting tone of a healthy muscle
The formation of new muscle fibers during recovery

quiz_questions.explanation: Atrophy is the wasting or reduction in muscle size that occurs when muscle fibers shrink due to decreased protein synthesis and increased protein degradation, caused by factors such as prolonged immobilization, nerve damage, aging (sarcopenia), or chronic disease.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is hypertrophy?

options.text: The accumulation of fat within muscle tissue
An increase in muscle size caused by enlargement of individual muscle fibers, typically from resistance training
The multiplication of muscle cells to create new fibers
The shrinking of muscle fibers due to inactivity

quiz_questions.explanation: Hypertrophy is the increase in skeletal muscle size that results from enlargement of existing muscle fibers (not creation of new ones) through increased synthesis of myofilaments and myofibrils, typically stimulated by resistance training and adequate nutrition.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is compartment syndrome?

options.text: A fracture that damages the bone marrow compartment
A dangerous condition where increased pressure within a closed muscle compartment compresses blood vessels and nerves, cutting off blood flow
An infection within a joint space
A benign condition that resolves without treatment

quiz_questions.explanation: Compartment syndrome occurs when swelling or bleeding within a closed fascial compartment raises the pressure to a level that compromises blood flow and nerve function, leading to ischemia and potential permanent tissue damage if not treated with emergency fasciotomy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is dystrophin?

options.text: A structural protein that connects the muscle fiber's cytoskeleton to the extracellular matrix, providing mechanical stability during contraction
An enzyme that breaks down lactic acid in muscles
A neurotransmitter released at the neuromuscular junction
A contractile protein that generates the power stroke

quiz_questions.explanation: Dystrophin is a large, rod-shaped cytoskeletal protein that forms a crucial link between the intracellular actin cytoskeleton and the extracellular matrix through the dystrophin-associated glycoprotein complex, protecting the sarcolemma from mechanical stress during contraction; its absence causes Duchenne muscular dystrophy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'autoimmune disorder' mean in the context of myasthenia gravis?

options.text: A condition in which the body's immune system mistakenly attacks its own tissues, in this case destroying ACh receptors at the NMJ
An infection by a virus that attacks the muscles
A genetic mutation that prevents immune cells from forming
A disorder caused by excessive exercise damaging the immune system

quiz_questions.explanation: An autoimmune disorder occurs when the immune system loses self-tolerance and generates antibodies against the body's own tissues; in myasthenia gravis, autoantibodies target and destroy nicotinic ACh receptors on the motor end plate, progressively reducing neuromuscular transmission.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'rhabdomyolysis' mean?

options.text: An inflammation of the joints surrounding muscles
The rapid breakdown (lysis) of damaged skeletal muscle (rhabdomyo-) releasing intracellular contents into the bloodstream
The growth of smooth muscle in the blood vessels
The normal breakdown and rebuilding of muscle during exercise

quiz_questions.explanation: Rhabdomyolysis literally means 'dissolution of striped (skeletal) muscle' and refers to the pathological destruction of muscle cells that releases potentially harmful intracellular contents (myoglobin, potassium, creatine kinase) into the blood, which can cause kidney failure and cardiac arrhythmias.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a muscle spasm?

options.text: The normal resting tone maintained by motor neurons
A sudden, involuntary, sustained contraction of a muscle or group of muscles that is often painful
Voluntary contraction performed during exercise
A complete tear of a muscle tendon

quiz_questions.explanation: A muscle spasm (or cramp) is an involuntary, sustained, and often painful contraction that occurs without conscious control, commonly caused by electrolyte imbalances, dehydration, muscle fatigue, or nerve irritation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'demyelination' mean in the context of multiple sclerosis and muscle weakness?

options.text: The destruction of the myelin sheath insulating nerve fibers, which slows or blocks nerve impulse transmission to muscles
The growth of new nerve fibers to replace damaged ones
The strengthening of the nerve coating to improve signal speed
The increase in muscle fiber size due to nerve stimulation

quiz_questions.explanation: Demyelination is the loss or destruction of the myelin sheath that normally insulates nerve fibers and speeds up signal conduction; in multiple sclerosis, the immune system attacks myelin on central nervous system neurons, slowing or blocking motor signals and causing muscle weakness, spasticity, and coordination problems.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'sprain' mean, and how does it differ from a strain?

options.text: A sprain and a strain are the same injury affecting muscles
A sprain is a bone fracture and a strain is a nerve injury
A sprain affects tendons and a strain affects ligaments
A sprain is an injury to a ligament (connecting bone to bone), while a strain is an injury to a muscle or tendon (connecting muscle to bone)

quiz_questions.explanation: A sprain involves the stretching or tearing of a ligament (the fibrous connective tissue connecting bones at a joint), such as an ankle sprain, while a strain involves the stretching or tearing of a muscle or its tendon, such as a hamstring strain.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 5-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician because his parents notice he uses his hands to 'walk up' his legs when standing from the floor (Gowers' sign). Genetic testing reveals an absence of dystrophin. What diagnosis should the physician suspect, and why does the child display this maneuver?

options.text: A simple muscle strain that will heal in a few weeks
Myasthenia gravis; the child lacks ACh receptors at the NMJ
Duchenne muscular dystrophy; without dystrophin, proximal muscles (especially hip and thigh extensors) weaken, forcing the child to use arm support to compensate
Fibromyalgia; the child is experiencing chronic pain without tissue damage

quiz_questions.explanation: Gowers' sign is a classic clinical indicator of Duchenne muscular dystrophy where the child uses the arms to 'climb up' the body when rising from the floor because the proximal hip and thigh muscles are severely weakened due to absence of dystrophin, which normally protects muscle fibers from contraction-induced damage.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 35-year-old woman reports that her eyelids droop progressively throughout the day and she has difficulty chewing by dinnertime, but symptoms improve after a night's rest. A neurologist suspects myasthenia gravis. Why do symptoms worsen with repeated use and improve with rest?

options.text: Her bones are weakening throughout the day from osteoporosis
With repeated nerve stimulation, the already-reduced ACh receptors become further depleted of available binding sites, but rest allows some recovery of neuromuscular transmission
Her muscles are being destroyed by a toxin that accumulates during the day
She has a muscle strain that worsens with movement

quiz_questions.explanation: In myasthenia gravis, autoantibodies have destroyed a significant portion of ACh receptors; with repeated stimulation, the limited remaining receptors become saturated and fewer end-plate potentials reach threshold, causing progressive weakness; rest allows ACh to accumulate and partial recovery of receptor availability.

quiz_questions.question_text: A college student starts an intense new workout program and develops severe muscle pain, swelling, and dark cola-colored urine 48 hours later. The emergency physician orders blood tests showing elevated creatine kinase and myoglobin. What condition does the student most likely have, and why is it dangerous?

options.text: Rhabdomyolysis, which is dangerous because released myoglobin can clog renal tubules and cause acute kidney failure
Normal DOMS that requires no treatment
A simple muscle strain requiring only rest and ice
Fibromyalgia triggered by exercise

quiz_questions.explanation: Dark urine (myoglobinuria), severe muscle pain, and markedly elevated creatine kinase are hallmarks of rhabdomyolysis, where extensive muscle breakdown releases myoglobin into the blood; myoglobin precipitates in renal tubules, and without aggressive IV fluid therapy, acute kidney failure can develop rapidly.

quiz_questions.question_text: A soldier steps on a rusty nail in a muddy field and does not receive proper wound care. Two weeks later, he develops jaw stiffness (lockjaw) and generalized muscle spasms. The field medic recognizes tetanus. How does the tetanus toxin cause these sustained muscle contractions?

options.text: The toxin destroys the sarcoplasmic reticulum, causing calcium to flood the muscle
The toxin directly stimulates muscle fibers to contract without any nerve involvement
The toxin increases acetylcholinesterase activity, causing muscle relaxation
The toxin blocks inhibitory neurotransmitter release (GABA and glycine) in the spinal cord, leaving motor neurons unchecked and causing sustained muscle contraction

quiz_questions.explanation: Tetanospasmin from Clostridium tetani travels retrogradely along motor neurons to the spinal cord, where it blocks the release of inhibitory neurotransmitters (GABA and glycine) from Renshaw cells and inhibitory interneurons, resulting in unopposed motor neuron firing and sustained, painful muscle spasms.

quiz_questions.question_text: A nurse is caring for an ICU patient who develops sudden, severe pain and swelling in his right lower leg after a tibial fracture was casted. The limb appears tense, and the patient reports numbness in the foot. The surgeon is urgently called. What is the likely diagnosis, and why is emergency surgery needed?

options.text: Fibromyalgia triggered by the injury
Acute compartment syndrome; the rising pressure within the fascial compartment is compressing blood vessels and nerves, and without emergency fasciotomy, the tissue will die from ischemia
Normal post-fracture swelling that requires only ice and elevation
A simple blood clot that can be treated with medication alone

quiz_questions.explanation: Acute compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency where swelling within a rigid fascial compartment raises pressure above capillary perfusion pressure, cutting off blood flow to muscles and nerves; without fasciotomy (surgical opening of the compartment) within 6 hours, irreversible muscle necrosis and nerve damage occur.

quiz_questions.question_text: A dermatologist is using Botox injections to treat a patient with chronic migraines. The patient asks how a toxin can be a medicine. Based on knowledge of muscular disorders, what is the therapeutic mechanism of Botox?

options.text: Botox increases blood flow to the brain by stimulating smooth muscle
Botox blocks ACh release at the NMJ in very small, targeted doses, temporarily relaxing overactive muscles and reducing the muscle tension that triggers migraines
Botox kills the bacteria that cause migraines
Botox strengthens the muscles around the head to prevent headaches

quiz_questions.explanation: Therapeutic Botox uses tiny, precisely targeted doses of botulinum toxin to block ACh release at selected neuromuscular junctions, causing localized, temporary muscle paralysis that reduces the excessive muscle tension contributing to chronic migraines, cervical dystonia, and spasticity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physical therapist is treating a patient with multiple sclerosis (MS) who is experiencing increasing weakness and spasticity in her legs. The patient asks why her muscles are getting weaker even though the disease affects nerves, not muscles. What is the best explanation?

options.text: MS destroys the myelin sheath on central nervous system motor neurons, impairing nerve signal transmission to muscles and causing weakness and spasticity even though the muscles themselves are initially healthy
MS is actually a muscle disease that has been misclassified
MS directly destroys the muscle fibers themselves
MS causes bones to weaken, which makes muscles appear weak

quiz_questions.explanation: In MS, the autoimmune destruction of myelin in the central nervous system slows or blocks motor signals traveling from the brain and spinal cord to skeletal muscles; although the muscles themselves are initially structurally normal, impaired innervation causes weakness, and damaged inhibitory pathways lead to spasticity.

quiz_questions.question_text: An elderly patient has been hospitalized for pneumonia and confined to bed for 3 weeks. Upon discharge, she is unable to walk unassisted. Her doctor explains that her leg muscles have atrophied. The patient asks if the muscle loss is permanent. What is the most accurate response?

options.text: Disuse atrophy is generally reversible with progressive exercise and physical therapy, as the muscle fibers can regrow and strengthen with appropriate stimulation
Yes, disuse atrophy is always permanent and irreversible
Surgery is the only way to restore atrophied muscles
The muscles were not actually lost; they just shrank temporarily and will return to normal size immediately

quiz_questions.explanation: Disuse atrophy is generally reversible because the muscle fibers shrink but are not destroyed; with progressive resistance exercise and physical therapy, protein synthesis increases, myofibrils are rebuilt, and muscle size and strength can be largely restored, though recovery may take longer in elderly patients.

quiz_questions.question_text: A weekend warrior participates in an unusually intense hiking trip after months of inactivity. Two days later, he can barely walk down stairs due to severe quadriceps soreness. Based on understanding of DOMS, what caused this delayed soreness and what type of contraction during hiking likely contributed most?

options.text: Lactic acid buildup during the hike caused permanent muscle damage
A bacterial infection entered the muscles during the hike
The concentric contractions during uphill climbing caused all of the soreness
Eccentric contractions of the quadriceps during downhill sections caused microscopic muscle fiber damage and an inflammatory repair response that peaks 24-72 hours later

quiz_questions.explanation: DOMS is primarily caused by eccentric contractions (which occur extensively during downhill walking when the quadriceps lengthens under load to control knee flexion), producing microscopic tears in muscle fibers and Z-line disruption, triggering an inflammatory response that causes pain peaking 24-72 hours post-exercise.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pediatric neurologist is counseling the mother of a boy newly diagnosed with Duchenne muscular dystrophy. The mother, who has no symptoms, asks why she is not affected by the disease if she carries the same mutation. What genetic explanation should the neurologist provide?

options.text: DMD only affects children under age 10 and adults are immune
The mother does not actually carry the mutation; it arose spontaneously in the child
The disease is caused by environmental factors, not genetics
As a female carrier, the mother has two X chromosomes; her normal X chromosome produces enough dystrophin to compensate for the mutated one, while her son has only one X chromosome with no backup

quiz_questions.explanation: Duchenne MD is X-linked recessive; female carriers have two X chromosomes, and the normal copy on one X produces sufficient dystrophin through random X-inactivation to maintain muscle function, while males have only one X chromosome and therefore express the full disease phenotype when the dystrophin gene is mutated.
